interpretation: FBA objective value represents the optimized model objective, usually biomass-related growth prediction. Published 13C-MFA datasets usually report estimated intracellular fluxes, not an optimized objective value.
Both methods can provide intracellular flux values, but FBA fluxes are computational predictions while 13C-MFA fluxes are experimentally constrained estimates.
Blocked reactions are a constraint-based model property and are not usually reported as an experimental 13C-MFA metric.
Both approaches can be used to study how metabolism changes under different carbon sources, but the comparison should focus on qualitative trends.
Both approaches can examine aerobic versus anaerobic metabolism when suitable datasets are available.
FBA directly predicts a biomass-related objective, while 13C-MFA may be associated with measured growth rates but does not optimize growth in the same way.
13C-MFA uses isotope-labeling measurements, while FBA predictions require external experimental data for validation.
